exploration_log.aha_moment: The fix is two lines: add `ThrowIfCancellationRequested()` before the `using` block and change the buffer size constant for async. The 4096 byte buffer means CopyToAsync will check cancellation at every 4KB read instead of every 80KB read.
exploration_log.hindsight: Nothing; the fix was straightforward from reading the code.

key_decisions.decision: Use 4096 bytes as the async buffer size
key_decisions.reasoning: 4096 is the standard I/O buffer size and matches what the .NET runtime uses for async stream copies internally; small enough for responsive cancellation without excessive syscall overhead

search_sequence.result: Found CopyToAsync uses DefaultBufferSize (80KB) and has no early cancellation check; also found DefaultBufferSize constant at line 12

solve_notes: Added `cancellationToken.ThrowIfCancellationRequested()` before the stream copy in `CopyToAsync` to provide early cancellation. Added a new `DefaultAsyncBufferSize = 4096` constant and switched `CopyToAsync` to use it instead of the 80KB `DefaultBufferSize`, so the cancellation check inside `CopyToAsync` fires more frequently during large file reads.
task_text: `FormFile.CopyToAsync()` in `src/Http/Http/src/FormFile.cs` passes the cancellation token to the underlying stream copy but does not check for cancellation before starting the copy. For large files, the cancellation may not take effect until the next buffer read. Add an early `cancellationToken.ThrowIfCancellationRequested()` check and reduce the default buffer size for more responsive cancellation.
